title: Schedule and runbook Dolt store maintenance (dolt gc + snapshot)
description:
Implements ADR 0002 (docs/adr/0002-dolt-store-maintenance-runbook.md) per architecture doc docs/architecture/gc-read-path.md (UC-3).

Scope: complementary to the read-path routing change. ADR 0001 fixes the hot path; this fixes the cold path.

Required design decisions for the designer:
1. Scheduler choice: gc formula vs deacon patrol vs an existing periodic hook. ADR 0002 mandates reusing an in-tree scheduler — pick which one.
2. Maintenance lease implementation. How does the scheduler signal 'no other writers right now'? Propose a concrete lease primitive (existing one if present).
3. dolt backup invocation against the managed Dolt server: subcommand, target path, naming convention, retention pruning policy. ADR 0002 says keep last 3 successful + most-recent failed.
4. dolt gc invocation: exact CLI form, expected lock duration, how to detect partial failure.
5. Smoke-test definition: what bd query proves the store is still readable post-gc.
6. Event payloads: gc.store.maintenance.done {duration_s, before_bytes, after_bytes}, gc.store.maintenance.failed {exit_code, snapshot_path}. Where is the event-emission helper? Reuse it.
7. Operator alert channel for failures: mail to which rig/agent? Confirm with the user if unsure.
8. Manual override command shape (must be documented, not hidden).
9. Disk-usage warning in gc status / gc citystatus: where exactly to add the check, and the precise threshold expression (initial: size_bytes > 1 MB × live_rows).
10. Initial schedule cadence (architecture doc says weekly off-peak as a starting point).

Inputs in repo:
- ADR: docs/adr/0002-dolt-store-maintenance-runbook.md
- Architecture doc: docs/architecture/gc-read-path.md (UC-3 flow)
- Rule: docs/rules/dolt-store-maintenance.md
- Live data point: .beads/dolt at 11G with ~64 live rows in gm db today
notes:
Derived from gc-read-path architecture (ADR 0002). Source bead: ga-1f9.
# Design: Dolt store maintenance runbook (ADR 0002)

**Designer:** gascity/designer
**Source bead:** ga-d5y
**Parent analysis:** ga-1f9 (architect)
**Date:** 2026-04-22

This document resolves the 10 open design decisions called out in the
bead description.

> **Note on agent role:** This bead is a runbook + scheduler design
> task with no UI component, so no wireframes. Diagrams below are
> Mermaid sequence/state, consistent with the architecture doc's
> existing UC-3 flow. No ASCII art.

---

## D1 — Scheduler choice: new `StoreMaintenanceLoop` inside supervisor

**Decision:** add a new `StoreMaintenanceLoop` goroutine inside the
supervisor process, sibling to `CachingStore.reconcileLoop`. Config
lives in `city.toml` under a new `[maintenance.dolt]` section.

**Why this, not formulas / deacon patrol / a new cron library:**

The exploration established that (a) `gc formula` is a TOML-driven
user narrative system — it does NOT have a periodic-trigger
primitive; (b) there is no "deacon patrol" periodic hook in-tree;
(c) the only prior art for periodic work is
`internal/beads/caching_store_reconcile.go::reconcileLoop` (a
goroutine with `time.Timer`). The honest interpretation of the rule
"reuse the factory's existing scheduler" is: follow the reconciler's
pattern. That is the scheduler we have.

**No new abstraction:** I deliberately do NOT introduce a generic
`Periodic` registry. YAGNI — one consumer today, introduce the
abstraction when the second arrives. Naming the new goroutine
`StoreMaintenanceLoop` (not `MaintenanceLoop`) keeps the scope
narrow and refactoring later is easy.

**Contract:**

```go
// internal/supervisor/maintenance.go (new)

type StoreMaintenanceLoop struct {
    cfg       config.DoltMaintenance  // from [maintenance.dolt]
    store     *beads.CachingStore
    cityPath  string
    recorder  events.Recorder
    mu        sync.Mutex              // D2 in-proc lease
    lastRunAt time.Time
    history   []MaintenanceRun        // bounded ring, size 16
}

func (m *StoreMaintenanceLoop) Run(ctx context.Context) {
    t := time.NewTimer(m.nextDelay(time.Now()))
    for {
        select {
        case <-ctx.Done(): return
        case <-t.C:
        }
        if m.nextDelay(time.Now()) == 0 {
            m.runOnce(ctx)  // respects lease; may skip if held
        }
        t.Reset(m.nextDelay(time.Now()))
    }
}
```

**Config:**

```toml
[maintenance.dolt]
enabled = true          # default false — opt-in for v1
interval = "168h"       # weekly; see D10
alert_to = ""           # see D7
```

`enabled = false` by default so landing this bead is safe on
existing cities. Operators turn it on once the runbook is published.

---

## D2 — Maintenance lease: in-proc mutex + API single-writer

**Decision:** the supervisor is the ONLY writer of maintenance runs.
The lease is an in-memory `sync.Mutex` on the
`StoreMaintenanceLoop`. Manual override (D8) routes through a new
API endpoint, so it contends on the same mutex — no cross-process
coordination needed.

If a second maintenance request arrives while one is running, the
API endpoint returns **HTTP 409 Conflict** with body
`{"type": "maintenance-in-progress", "started_at": "..."}`. The
loop skips its scheduled tick if the mutex is held.

**Why not a filesystem lock (flock / PID file):**

- Filesystem locks require the manual-override CLI to be prepared
  to handle lock-held state anyway; moving that logic to the API
  centralizes it in one place.
- PID files violate the "No status files — query live state"
  principle in AGENTS.md.
- The scheduler lives in one process by design (D1). Cross-process
  contention only arises for the override path — and the override
  path we route through the same process.

**If supervisor is down AND operator wants to run maintenance
manually:** the CLI errors with exit 2 and tells the operator to
start the controller first. Running `dolt gc` directly bypasses the
snapshot-first invariant and the event emission — do not support it
as a sanctioned path. If an emergency demands it, operators can
invoke `dolt gc` manually against the Dolt server; they're outside
the documented procedure.

---

## D3 — Snapshot via `dolt backup`: path convention and retention

**Decision:** use `dolt backup` as mandated by ADR 0002. Each
maintenance run creates a timestamped snapshot directory under
`<city>/.beads/dolt-backups/`.

**Invocation contract (builder verifies exact Dolt CLI against
vendored version):**

1. On first run, ensure a named backup target exists:
   ```
   dolt backup add snapshot-target file://<city>/.beads/dolt-backups/current
   ```
   Idempotent — if it exists, skip. The address points to a
   "working" location that gets rotated after each push.
2. Push snapshot:
   ```
   dolt backup sync snapshot-target
   ```
   (or the equivalent subcommand in the vendored Dolt — `push`,
   `sync`, or `backup` depending on version. Builder confirms.)
3. Move `current/` to `<timestamp>/` where `<timestamp>` is
   `2026-04-22T03-00-00Z` (no colons so it's valid on all
   filesystems). This becomes the immutable retained snapshot.

**Path convention:**

```
<city>/.beads/dolt-backups/
  success/
    2026-04-15T03-00-00Z/
    2026-04-08T03-00-00Z/
    2026-04-01T03-00-00Z/
  failed/
    2026-04-22T03-00-00Z/    (at most 1 kept)
  current/                    (work area; ephemeral)
```

**Retention policy:** after each successful run, scan `success/`,
sort by directory name (ISO timestamp sorts correctly), keep the
newest 3, remove the rest. After each failed run, scan `failed/`,
keep the newest 1, remove older.

Pruning runs in the same goroutine after the run completes. Retention
errors are logged but do NOT fail the run — a failed prune shouldn't
roll back a successful gc.

**Why not hardlink / filesystem snapshots (btrfs, zfs):** portable
across filesystems; matches the ADR's explicit mandate for
`dolt backup`.

**Known risk:** `dolt backup` semantics may differ between versions.
The builder bead MUST include a verification step that confirms the
backup can be restored via `dolt backup restore` on a throwaway
directory before trusting it as a real snapshot. Include this step
in the test plan.

---

## D4 — `dolt gc` invocation: `CALL DOLT_GC()` via SQL

**Decision:** invoke gc as a SQL stored procedure against the
running managed Dolt server:

```
CALL DOLT_GC();
```

Not the CLI `dolt gc` subcommand — that requires exclusive access
and conflicts with the managed-server invariant ("cannot tolerate
long exclusive locks").

**Why the SQL form:**

- `CALL DOLT_GC()` is online: it runs inside the server process,
  coordinates with live connections, and doesn't require stopping
  the server.
- It returns a row/error on completion — partial failure is
  detectable by checking the SQL error.
- The supervisor already holds SQL connection code via the beads
  layer; reuse the same connection path.

**Invocation path:**

```go
// internal/supervisor/maintenance.go
func (m *StoreMaintenanceLoop) runDoltGC(ctx context.Context) error {
    conn, err := m.store.DoltSQLConn(ctx)  // reuse existing conn factory
    if err != nil { return fmt.Errorf("acquiring SQL conn: %w", err) }
    defer conn.Close()
    ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(ctx, m.cfg.GCTimeout) // e.g., 10m
    defer cancel()
    if _, err := conn.ExecContext(ctx, "CALL DOLT_GC()"); err != nil {
        return fmt.Errorf("CALL DOLT_GC(): %w", err)
    }
    return nil
}
```

**Expected lock duration:** bounded by bead count. For ~221 live
beads with ~11 GB of chunk history, empirically 10-60 s on typical
hardware. Config `gc_timeout = "10m"` as initial ceiling; if we
ever hit it, something is very wrong.

**Partial failure detection:**
- SQL error returned → failure. Classify as `stage=gc`, attach
  error string to the `StoreMaintenanceFailed` event (D6).
- Timeout (context deadline exceeded) → failure. Snapshot retained;
  operator gets the alert. Manual inspection required because the
  server may be in a degraded state.
- Silent corruption (gc succeeds but store is broken) → caught by
  the smoke test in D5. On smoke-test failure, classify
  `stage=smoke-test`.

**Reject:** offline gc via `dolt gc` CLI. It would require stopping
the server for minutes — incompatible with a live factory.

---

## D5 — Smoke test: `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM dolt_beads LIMIT 1`

**Decision:** after a successful `CALL DOLT_GC()`, run a fast read
against the same SQL connection:

```sql
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM <beads-table-name> LIMIT 1
```

Where the table name is the one the beads layer already uses. If
the result is ≥ 1 and returns within 5 seconds, the store is
readable. Anything else (error, timeout, 0 rows) fails the smoke
test.

**Why not shell out to `bd list --json --limit 1`:**

- `bd` would open a new Dolt SQL server connection — doesn't
  exercise the same path as the supervisor's reads.
- Running inside the supervisor connection catches gc-induced
  corruption of that specific connection's view.
- Faster (no subprocess spawn).

**Why not a full list:** we're checking "is the store readable?",
not correctness of results. COUNT(*) is the cheapest query that
touches the chunk graph.

**Also useful:** on smoke-test failure, the supervisor attempts
`CachingStore.Reconcile()` and logs whether the cache agrees. If
the cache view diverges from the fresh SQL query, that's strong
signal of gc corruption. Not required for v1; note as a follow-up.

---

## D6 — Event payloads: reuse `events.Record` + `RegisterPayload`

**Decision:** two new event types registered through the existing
`events.RegisterPayload` helper in `internal/api/event_payloads.go`:

```go
// internal/events/events.go — add constants
const (
    StoreMaintenanceDone   Type = "gc.store.maintenance.done"
    StoreMaintenanceFailed Type = "gc.store.maintenance.failed"
)

// add to KnownEventTypes so the typed-wire invariant holds

// internal/events/payloads.go — new types
type StoreMaintenanceDonePayload struct {
    DurationSeconds float64 `json:"duration_s"`
    BeforeBytes     int64   `json:"before_bytes"`
    AfterBytes      int64   `json:"after_bytes"`
    SnapshotPath    string  `json:"snapshot_path"`
}

type StoreMaintenanceFailedPayload struct {
    Stage        string  `json:"stage"`          // "backup" | "gc" | "smoke-test" | "prune"
    ErrorMsg     string  `json:"error_msg"`
    SnapshotPath string  `json:"snapshot_path,omitempty"`  // empty if failure was before snapshot
    DurationSeconds float64 `json:"duration_s"`
}

// internal/api/event_payloads.go — registration
events.RegisterPayload(events.StoreMaintenanceDone,   StoreMaintenanceDonePayload{})
events.RegisterPayload(events.StoreMaintenanceFailed, StoreMaintenanceFailedPayload{})
```

**Caller (one per run):**

```go
// internal/supervisor/maintenance.go
m.recorder.Record(events.Event{
    Type:  events.StoreMaintenanceDone,
    Actor: "supervisor:store-maintenance",
    Data:  StoreMaintenanceDonePayload{...},
})
```

The existing invariant enforced by
`TestEveryKnownEventTypeHasRegisteredPayload` (AGENTS.md) catches
typos at CI time.

**`events.Recorder` location:** `openCityRecorder(stderr)` at
`cmd/gc/main.go:650` is CLI-side. Supervisor-side, the recorder is
constructed at supervisor bootstrap — builder threads it through to
the maintenance loop constructor, same as the caching store gets
threaded today.

---

## D7 — Operator alert channel: configurable `alert_to`, default off

**NEEDS USER CONFIRMATION.**

**Decision:** the alert target is **configurable via
`[maintenance.dolt] alert_to = "<agent-or-rig>"`**. No default
target — empty means "no mail, but the failure event still fires
and any subscriber (including `gc citystatus`) can surface it."

**Rationale:** the factory has no canonical "ops" rig. Forcing one
creates an infrastructure dependency that doesn't exist. The user
has three reasonable choices for where alerts go:

| Option | Target | Pro | Con |
|--------|--------|-----|-----|
| (A) dedicated ops rig | `gc-management/ops` (new rig) | Explicit on-call | Requires creating the rig |
| (B) the city's mayor | `gascity/mayor` | Mayor is the overseer; already exists | Mayor may be busy with planning |
| (C) deacon / supervisor role | `gascity/deacon` or `gascity/supervisor` | Infra-adjacent role | May not route to a human |

**My recommendation if forced to pick:** option B (mayor). Matches
the factory's existing escalation pattern — mayor sees problems
first, decides who fixes them.

**Please confirm which the user wants as the default in
`packs/actual/city.toml` / the gascity defaults.** Until confirmed,
I leave `alert_to = ""` and document the three options in the
runbook.

**Send mechanism:** `beadmail.Provider.Send(from, to, subject, body)`
(signature found at `internal/mail/beadmail/beadmail.go:30`).
From: `gascity/supervisor`. Subject:
`[ALERT] Dolt store maintenance failed: <stage>`. Body: full
failure payload as structured text + snapshot path for recovery.

**Mail is best-effort.** If mail send fails, log at error and
continue. Do NOT block maintenance-loop recovery on mail
deliverability.

---

## D8 — Manual override: `gc maintenance dolt-gc` CLI + API

**Decision:** two new subcommands and one new API endpoint.

### CLI

```
gc maintenance status           # show last run, next scheduled run, snapshot list
gc maintenance dolt-gc [--wait] # trigger a run now, default async; --wait blocks for completion
```

`dolt-gc` (not `run` or `gc`) so the command name reads as
"the dolt-gc flavor of maintenance" and is forward-compatible with
future maintenance types.

**Flags:**

- `--wait` (default false): block until the run completes, then
  print the result summary. Without `--wait`, returns immediately
  after the API accepts the request.
- `--json`: structured output for both subcommands.

**Exit codes:**

- 0: success (or accepted, if async)
- 1: gc failed (only with `--wait`)
- 2: supervisor not running / API unreachable
- 3: maintenance already in progress (HTTP 409 from API)

### API

New Huma handler at `/v0/city/{city}/maintenance/dolt-gc`:

- `POST` — trigger a run. Returns 202 Accepted with run-id for
  async, or 200 OK with full summary if `?wait=true`. 409 Conflict
  if already running.
- `GET /v0/city/{city}/maintenance/status` — current status + last
  N runs from the ring buffer.

The CLI routes through `apiClient(cityPath) + ShouldFallback`
exactly like any mutator (consistent with ga-h6w's pattern).

**"Documented, not hidden":** the command appears in `gc
maintenance --help` and in a new `docs/runbooks/dolt-maintenance.md`
section. No `--expert` or `--danger` flag gating.

**No `--skip-snapshot` flag in v1.** Ever. If an operator wants to
run `dolt gc` without a snapshot, they can do so manually against
the Dolt server, bypassing this code path entirely. Having the
flag at all invites misuse.

---

## D9 — Disk-usage warning in `gc status` / `gc citystatus`

**Decision:** extend `StatusJSON` / `StatusSummaryJSON` in
`cmd/gc/cmd_citystatus.go` with a new `StoreHealth` sub-struct.
Human output gains a "Store Health" line; JSON output gains a
nested field.

**JSON shape (additive):**

```go
// cmd/gc/cmd_citystatus.go
type StoreHealth struct {
    Path        string  `json:"path"`          // <city>/.beads/dolt
    SizeBytes   int64   `json:"size_bytes"`
    LiveRows    int     `json:"live_rows"`
    RatioMB     float64 `json:"ratio_mb_per_row"` // size_bytes / 1e6 / live_rows
    Warning     bool    `json:"warning"`
    ThresholdMB float64 `json:"threshold_mb_per_row"` // default 1.0
    LastGCAt    string  `json:"last_gc_at,omitempty"`   // ISO-8601
    LastGCStatus string `json:"last_gc_status,omitempty"` // "success" | "failed" | ""
}

// Extend StatusSummaryJSON:
type StatusSummaryJSON struct {
    // ... existing fields ...
    StoreHealth *StoreHealth `json:"store_health,omitempty"`
}
```

Nested under `store_health` so a consumer can check
`resp.summary?.store_health?.warning` without breaking if the field
is missing (older supervisor).

**Threshold expression:** `warning = size_bytes > 1_000_000 *
live_rows` (1 MB per row — ADR-specified initial value). Expose
`ThresholdMB` in the JSON so operators can see what threshold is
tripping without reading source.

**Computation location:** in `doCityStatus`, at the same point the
summary block is built. Two new calls:
- `os.Stat` + recursive walk of `<city>/.beads/dolt` to get size
  (cache the result with a 30 s TTL in the supervisor; the walk is
  fast but not free).
- `caching-store.Count()` or equivalent for live row count.

**Last run data source:** the supervisor's ring buffer (`history`)
from D1. The API `GET /.../status` response carries it; the CLI
renders it.

**Human output (new section, appended after existing summary):**

```
Store health:
  Path:        /path/to/.beads/dolt
  Size:        11.2 GB
  Live rows:   221
  Ratio:       50.7 MB/row  (threshold 1.0 MB/row)  ⚠ maintenance overdue
  Last GC:     2026-04-01T03:00:00Z (success)
```

When no warning, drop the `⚠` and the "maintenance overdue" text.

**`gc status` parity:** `cmd_status.go` renders the rig-scoped
view but the Store Health block is city-level. Add it to
`gc citystatus` only; `gc status` gets a one-line pointer if it
shows city health today. (Builder confirms current `gc status`
shape; if it already includes city-level summary, mirror there
too.)

---

## D10 — Initial cadence: weekly, interval-based

**Decision:** `interval = "168h"` (7 days) with no time-of-day
targeting. First run fires `interval` after controller startup, or
immediately on startup if `last_run_at` is absent / >1.5× interval
ago.

**Why no time-of-day:** adding "Sunday 03:00 UTC" logic requires a
cron parser, timezone handling, and DST edge cases. One-line
`time.Duration` interval is robust. Off-peak is less critical once
maintenance is behaving — if the v1 interval is noisy, operators
tune or run manually (D8) at their chosen window.

**Follow-up bead (not this one):** add `preferred_time_utc =
"sun.10:00"` config once we have a week of data showing whether
interval-based timing is actually problematic. Until then: YAGNI.

**Jitter:** add ±10 % jitter on the next-due calculation so that
multiple cities on one host don't all fire at the same minute.
Jitter is trivial (`interval * (0.9 + 0.2*rand.Float64())`) and
avoids an easy foot-gun.

**State persistence:** `last_run_at` persists via the event log
(events are already durable). On startup, the loop queries the
most recent `StoreMaintenanceDone` event for its own city and uses
that timestamp. No separate state file.

---

## State machine — maintenance run (complements UC-3 from arch doc)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> AcquireLease: timer fires OR manual POST
    AcquireLease --> Idle: lease held → skip / 409
    AcquireLease --> Snapshot: mutex acquired
    Snapshot --> DoltGC: dolt backup sync OK
    Snapshot --> EmitFailed: backup error (stage=backup)
    DoltGC --> SmokeTest: CALL DOLT_GC() OK
    DoltGC --> EmitFailed: sql error (stage=gc)
    SmokeTest --> Prune: COUNT OK
    SmokeTest --> EmitFailed: count error/timeout (stage=smoke-test)
    Prune --> EmitDone: retention applied
    Prune --> EmitDone: prune error logged but non-fatal
    EmitDone --> ReleaseLease
    EmitFailed --> AlertOperator: mail if alert_to set
    AlertOperator --> RetainSnapshot
    RetainSnapshot --> ReleaseLease
    ReleaseLease --> Idle
```

---

## Runbook text (to be added to `docs/runbooks/dolt-maintenance.md`)

> **Note:** This runbook is produced by the **rig maintainer**, not
> by this designer agent. I provide the content; the pm + writer
> land the file.

Outline for the rig maintainer / pm:

1. **What this runs.** `CALL DOLT_GC()` + `dolt backup sync` in the
   supervisor process, weekly.
2. **Opt-in.** Set `[maintenance.dolt] enabled = true` in
   `city.toml`. Restart the controller.
3. **Alerting.** Set `alert_to = "<agent-id>"` for mail on failure.
4. **Observability.** `gc maintenance status` or `gc citystatus` →
   "Store health" block.
5. **Manual trigger.** `gc maintenance dolt-gc --wait`.
6. **Emergency rollback.** If gc corrupts the store, stop the
   controller, restore from the most recent
   `<city>/.beads/dolt-backups/success/<timestamp>/` using
   `dolt clone` or `dolt backup restore`, restart.
7. **Disabling.** `enabled = false` + restart.

The pm decomposes this outline into a standalone rig-maintainer
bead (written by the writer role if that exists, or handled by
the pm directly).

---

## Open items for user / pm confirmation

1. **D7 alert target:** pick A/B/C above. Recommended B
   (`gascity/mayor`) unless the user has a different convention.
2. **D3 Dolt CLI form:** the exact `dolt backup` subcommand depends
   on the vendored Dolt version. Builder must verify as part of
   the runbook bead's test plan.
3. **D10 cadence:** is 168 h (7 days) right for this factory, or
   should v1 start at 336 h (14 days) and tighten after data?
   Weekly is ADR-suggested; open if user has a different baseline.
4. **`maintenance.dolt.enabled` default:** I chose `false` (opt-in).
   Confirm the user wants opt-in rather than opt-out for the v1
   rollout.
5. **Runbook file location:** I suggested
   `docs/runbooks/dolt-maintenance.md` since `docs/rules/dolt-store-
   maintenance.md` already exists and is the rule (the "why"). The
   runbook (the "how, step-by-step") deserves its own file. pm
   confirms naming.

## Out of scope (deliberately)

- Cross-city maintenance coordination. Each city's supervisor
  handles its own store. Multi-city throttling, if ever needed,
  is a separate design.
- Automatic recovery from corruption (e.g., auto-restore from
  snapshot). Manual intervention only for v1; auto-recovery
  introduces its own risks.
- Compaction metrics dashboard. A single ring-buffered history
  visible via `gc maintenance status` covers ops needs for v1.

## Handoff breakdown for pm

Suggested bead decomposition (roughly ordered):

| # | Bead scope | Depends on |
|---|-----------|------------|
| 1 | `[maintenance.dolt]` config section + parser + defaults | — |
| 2 | `StoreMaintenanceLoop` skeleton + lease mutex + lifecycle wiring | 1 |
| 3 | Snapshot step (`dolt backup` invocation + path convention + retention) | 2 |
| 4 | `CALL DOLT_GC()` step + smoke test | 2 |
| 5 | Event registration + emission (Done / Failed) | 2 |
| 6 | Operator alert mail path | 5 |
| 7 | `gc maintenance dolt-gc` + `gc maintenance status` CLI + API handler | 2,3,4,5 |
| 8 | `StoreHealth` block in `gc citystatus` | 2,5 |
| 9 | Runbook doc + enabling instructions | 1-8 |

Beads 3, 4, 5, 8 can be parallel after 2 lands. Bead 9 is last.

No bead in this decomposition blocks or is blocked by ga-h6w (the
read-path routing design) — the two designs are independent.
close_reason: Decomposition complete — 9 children routed to gascity/builder; config bead ga-zol slung.

title: Wire gc read-path CLI commands through supervisor API with fallback
description:
Implements ADR 0001 (docs/adr/0001-gc-read-path-routes-through-supervisor.md) per architecture doc docs/architecture/gc-read-path.md.

Scope: 16 read-path commands in cmd/gc/cmd_*.go (session list/peek, mail check/peek, rig list, convoy list/status/check, wait list/inspect, citystatus, status, order history, bead reads).

Required design decisions for the designer:
1. Exact API surface to add to internal/api/client.go — a typed wrapper per read command (e.g. ListSessions, ListMail, ListRigs) vs a thinner readClient helper. Architecture doc recommends per-command wrappers; revisit if surface > ~10.
2. Decoder/translator layer between genclient generated types and CLI-facing types. The rule forbids leaking genclient.* into cmd/gc/.
3. Logging shape for route=api|fallback reason=<code>. Reuse existing diagnostic level conventions in cmd/gc/.
4. Operator escape-hatch flag/env var (rule mandates it). Choose the name (architecture doc suggests GC_NO_API but defers to designer).
5. Cache-staleness handling: should --json output include a meta field like _cache_age_s? The architecture doc lists this as an open question.
6. Order in which the 16 commands are migrated (suggest a single bead-per-command or grouped by file).
7. Test plan: each routed command needs alive / down / non-fallbackable-error coverage. Confirm fixtures use api.NewCityScopedClient pointed at a httptest.Server (see cmd_events_test.go for prior art).
8. Handler MUST refuse to serve when cache.IsLive() == false; classify that refusal as fallbackable. Decide the precise HTTP status / error code.

Inputs in repo:
- ADR: docs/adr/0001-gc-read-path-routes-through-supervisor.md
- Architecture doc: docs/architecture/gc-read-path.md (sections AF-1, AF-2 are the per-call contract)
- Rule: docs/rules/gc-read-path-routes-through-api.md
- Existing prior art: cmd/gc/cmd_suspend.go:65, cmd/gc/cmd_agent.go:552, cmd/gc/apiroute.go:20
- Generated client: internal/api/genclient/client_gen.go (GetV0CityByCityNameSessions, GetV0CityByCityNameBeads, etc.)
- CachingStore: internal/beads/caching_store.go
notes:
Derived from gc-read-path architecture (ADR 0001). Source bead: ga-1f9.
# Design: gc read-path routing through supervisor API (ADR 0001)

**Designer:** gascity/designer
**Source bead:** ga-h6w
**Parent analysis:** ga-1f9 (architect)
**Date:** 2026-04-22

This document resolves the 8 open design decisions called out in the
bead description. Decisions are final unless flagged "needs user
confirmation." Each decision includes the reasoning, the concrete
contract the builder must hit, and (where useful) short code sketches
so the pm can decompose into buildable beads.

> **Note on agent role:** This bead is an engineering-design task with
> no UI component, so there are no wireframes. The design artifacts are
> the decisions below plus two new Mermaid sequence diagrams that
> complement (not replace) the architecture doc's AF-1/AF-2 diagrams.
> No ASCII art.

---

## D1 — API surface shape: per-command wrappers on `*api.Client`

**Decision:** Each newly routed read command gets a typed method on
`*api.Client` that returns a stable CLI-facing type, mirroring the
existing mutator pattern (`SuspendCity`, `SuspendAgent`, etc.).

**Reject:** a thin `readClient` helper that would call genclient
directly and decode at each call site. That approach (a) forces every
call site to know about genclient envelopes, (b) violates the rule
forbidding genclient leakage into `cmd/gc/`, and (c) introduces a
second style inconsistent with the mutator precedent.

**Revisit:** the surface is bounded at 16 wrappers today; this sits
inside the architecture doc's "≤ ~10" heuristic by file-grouping
(see D6). If the count grows past ~25 wrappers, revisit whether a
declarative dispatcher makes more sense.

**Contract for the builder:**

```go
// internal/api/client.go (additions)

func (c *Client) ListSessions(ctx context.Context, state, template string) ([]SessionSummary, error) {
    if err := c.requireCityScope(); err != nil { return nil, err }
    params := &genclient.GetV0CityByCityNameSessionsParams{}
    if state != "" { params.State = &state }
    if template != "" { params.Template = &template }
    resp, err := c.cw.GetV0CityByCityNameSessionsWithResponse(ctx, c.cityName, params)
    if err != nil { return nil, wrapConn(err) }
    return decodeSessions(resp) // translator in internal/api/decode_sessions.go
}
```

Each wrapper: (a) scope check, (b) parameter translation, (c) HTTP
call, (d) error classification via `wrapConn` / `classifyStatus`,
(e) decode through a translator function.

---

## D2 — Translator layer: `internal/api/decode_*.go`

**Decision:** one translator function per endpoint, living in
`internal/api/decode_<domain>.go` (e.g., `decode_sessions.go`,
`decode_mail.go`). Each translator accepts the generated
`*genclient.Get…Response` and returns the stable CLI type plus any
typed error.

CLI-facing types live in `internal/api/types_read.go` (new file) next
to the existing mutator types, grouped by domain:

```go
// internal/api/types_read.go
type SessionSummary struct {
    ID        string    `json:"id"`
    Template  string    `json:"template"`
    State     string    `json:"state"`
    StartedAt time.Time `json:"started_at"`
    // ... full shape derived from existing BdStore formatter
}
```

**Rule:** translators MUST be covered by unit tests with at least
(a) a valid response, (b) an empty list, (c) a partial/missing
field. `internal/api/decode_sessions_test.go` pattern.

**Why separate files per domain:** keeps `client.go` free of 200+
lines of field mapping; makes it easy to audit a translator without
scrolling past unrelated ones.

**Cross-reference:** the existing `BdStore`-backed formatter code
(e.g., `cmd/gc/cmd_session.go::formatSessionList`) defines the
canonical human-facing shape. The CLI-facing struct in `internal/api`
MUST match it 1:1 so human and JSON output remain identical whether
served via API or fallback.

---

## D3 — Logging shape: `route=<api|fallback> reason=<code>`

**Decision:** single log line per routed read, emitted immediately
after routing is determined, at the same log level existing `cmd/gc/`
diagnostics use (typically via `diag(...)` / writes to stderr behind
`GC_DEBUG=1`).

Format (key=value to match existing diag style):

```
route=api cmd=session-list city=gc-management age_s=3.2
route=fallback cmd=session-list city=gc-management reason=controller-down
route=fallback cmd=session-list city=gc-management reason=conn-refused
route=fallback cmd=session-list city=gc-management reason=cache-not-live
route=fallback cmd=session-list city=gc-management reason=read-only
route=fallback cmd=session-list city=gc-management reason=client-init
route=fallback cmd=session-list city=gc-management reason=escape-hatch
```

**Reason codes (closed set):**

| Code | Condition |
|------|-----------|
| `controller-down` | `controllerAlive(cityPath) == 0` |
| `conn-refused` | genclient returns net connection error |
| `cache-not-live` | handler returned 503 with typed `cache-not-live` body (see D8) |
| `read-only` | `readOnlyError` — shouldn't normally occur on reads but preserves symmetry |
| `client-init` | `clientInitError` from `apiClient(cityPath)` |
| `escape-hatch` | `GC_NO_API=1` set (see D4) |
| `non-loopback-bind` | supervisor bound non-loopback without `allow_mutations` |

A non-fallbackable error does NOT emit `route=fallback`; it prints
the error and exits non-zero (rule §4). The log line in that case is
implicit in the stderr error surface.

**Helper:** add `cmd/gc/apiroute.go::logRoute(cmd, route, reason
string, extra ...any)` so every call site uses the same format.
Regression test: grep-based asserting every routed command has exactly
one `logRoute(...)` call per exit path.

---

## D4 — Operator escape hatch: `GC_NO_API=1`

**Decision:** the environment variable **`GC_NO_API`** disables API
routing. Any truthy value (`1`, `true`, `yes`, case-insensitive) is
truthy; empty or unset is falsy. Other values reject at startup with
a one-line warning and continue as if unset (fail-open for an ops
var; fail-closed would turn a typo into an outage).

**Where checked:** first line of `apiroute.apiClient(cityPath)`:

```go
func apiClient(cityPath string) *api.Client {
    if envBoolTrue("GC_NO_API") {
        logRoute("", "fallback", "escape-hatch")
        return nil
    }
    if controllerAlive(cityPath) == 0 { /* ... existing ... */ }
    // ...
}
```

Checking at the gate (not per-command) ensures every routed command
honors the flag uniformly and emits the escape-hatch log line exactly
once per command.

**Rejected:** a CLI flag (`--no-api`). Per-command flags would force
every command to declare the flag and remember to plumb it. Env var
is process-global, matches debugging workflow ("set it in your shell,
run whatever"), and matches the architecture doc's suggestion.

**Documentation:** add a short section to `docs/rules/gc-read-path-
routes-through-api.md` under "Operator escape hatch." That's the
rule's job; no man page change needed.

---

## D5 — Cache-staleness signal: `_cache_age_s` envelope field

**Decision:** every API-path response from a routed read command
includes a top-level `_cache_age_s` field (float, seconds since
`CachingStore.lastReconcile` for that data). Fallback-path responses
omit the field entirely.

**Shape:**

```jsonc
// gc session list --json (api path)
{
  "_cache_age_s": 3.2,
  "sessions": [ {"id": "...", ... } ]
}

// gc session list --json (fallback path)
{
  "sessions": [ {"id": "...", ... } ]
}
```

The field sits at the envelope level so it works for both list and
single-object responses without per-type changes.

**Human output:** no banner for ages < 30 s. Above 30 s, a single
line appended AFTER the rendered table:

```
(cache age: 42s — reconciler may be lagging)
```

This banner does not appear on the fallback path.

**Why always-include on API path (not "only when > N"):** the JSON
schema becomes stable — consumers can branch on presence/absence to
detect the path. Variable-presence within the API path would be a
footgun (script works, then one day breaks when cache is "too fresh").

**Needs user confirmation:** if any existing `gc … --json` consumer
relies on a closed JSON schema (no extra fields allowed), the field
addition is a breaking change for them. Flag for the pm to raise
with the user before the enabler bead lands.

---

## D6 — Migration order: one enabler bead + 9 per-file beads

**Decision:** decompose into 10 beads, landed sequentially:

| Order | Bead scope | Commands |
|-------|-----------|----------|
| 1 | **Enabler** | `api.Client` read-wrapper scaffolding, `GC_NO_API` gate, `route=…` logger, `_cache_age_s` envelope, `cache-not-live` typed error + `ShouldFallback` classification, handler `IsLive` gate (see D8) |
| 2 | `cmd_session.go` | session list, session peek |
| 3 | `cmd_mail.go` | mail check, mail peek, mail count |
| 4 | `cmd_rig.go` | rig list |
| 5 | `cmd_convoy.go` | convoy list, convoy status, convoy check |
| 6 | `cmd_wait.go` | wait list, wait inspect |
| 7 | `cmd_citystatus.go` + `cmd_status.go` | citystatus, status (share `/status` handler) |
| 8 | `cmd_order.go` | order history |
| 9 | `cmd_beads.go` | bead list, bead show |

**Why grouped by file, not by command:** each file's commands
share fixtures, CLI flag parsing, and table-driven test scaffolding.
Grouping amortizes the PR overhead. Each bead still produces a
reviewable diff under ~300 lines.

**Dependencies:** beads 2-9 each `blocks` nothing but `blocked-by`
bead 1 (the enabler). Beads 2-9 can land in any order after 1.

**Bead 7 grouping note:** `citystatus` and `status` both consume the
supervisor's existing status endpoint and share rendering code. Keep
them together; splitting them just means two PRs both touching the
same test file.

**Cold-path floor:** none of these beads block ADR 0002 (ga-d5y).
ADR 0001 fixes the hot path; ADR 0002 fixes the cold path. They are
independent and can land in parallel.

---

## D7 — Test plan: `httptest.Server` + `api.NewCityScopedClient`

**Decision:** every routed command bead gets a table-driven test
using `httptest.NewServer` wired to `api.NewCityScopedClient(url,
city)`. No new test infrastructure.

**Prior art to adapt:** `cmd/gc/cmd_events_test.go::newEventsTestServer`
has the `httptest.NewServer(http.HandlerFunc(...))` shape. Extend
that pattern — but add a `routeTable` parameter so each test row can
install a custom response for its scenario.

**Table-driven shape (mandatory rows):**

```go
tests := []struct {
    name            string
    serverHandler   http.HandlerFunc  // nil => no server (simulates controller down)
    useEscapeHatch  bool              // set GC_NO_API=1
    wantExitCode    int
    wantStdoutRE    *regexp.Regexp
    wantStderrRE    *regexp.Regexp
    wantRouteLog    string             // e.g. "route=api"
}{
    {name: "api-happy-path",     serverHandler: okSessionsHandler,        wantExitCode: 0, wantRouteLog: "route=api"},
    {name: "api-cache-not-live", serverHandler: cacheNotLiveHandler,      wantExitCode: 0, wantRouteLog: "route=fallback reason=cache-not-live"},
    {name: "api-500-fallback",   serverHandler: fiveHundredHandler,       wantExitCode: 0, wantRouteLog: "route=fallback reason=conn-refused"},
    {name: "api-404-error",      serverHandler: notFoundHandler,          wantExitCode: 1, wantStderrRE: regexp.MustCompile("not found")},
    {name: "controller-down",    serverHandler: nil,                      wantExitCode: 0, wantRouteLog: "route=fallback reason=controller-down"},
    {name: "escape-hatch",       serverHandler: okSessionsHandler,        useEscapeHatch: true, wantExitCode: 0, wantRouteLog: "route=fallback reason=escape-hatch"},
}
```

**Fallback assertions:** when `wantRouteLog` starts with
`route=fallback`, the test ALSO asserts the fallback code path
produced correct output — via a stub `BdStore` injected through the
existing fsys abstraction or a `t.Setenv` pointing at a fixture.
This catches the regression where routing "fell back" but the
fallback path itself was broken.

**Coverage gate:** the enabler bead adds a CI lint asserting each
`cmd_*_test.go` with a routed command has all six named rows. Simple
grep in `scripts/` is enough; no new Go test framework.

---

## D8 — Cache-not-live HTTP contract

**Decision:** when `CachingStore.IsLive() == false`, read handlers
return **HTTP 503 Service Unavailable** with a typed Huma error body:

```json
{
  "status": 503,
  "title": "Cache not yet live",
  "detail": "Supervisor cache is priming or reconciling; retry via fallback.",
  "type": "cache-not-live"
}
```

`api.ShouldFallback` gains a new clause:

```go
// internal/api/client.go
type cacheNotLiveError struct{ httpStatus int }
func (e *cacheNotLiveError) Error() string { return "cache not live" }

func ShouldFallback(err error) bool {
    if IsConnError(err) { return true }
    var ro *readOnlyError;       if errors.As(err, &ro) { return true }
    var ci *clientInitError;     if errors.As(err, &ci) { return true }
    var cnl *cacheNotLiveError;  if errors.As(err, &cnl) { return true }
    return false
}
```

Translator/classifier: when the generated client returns a 503 with
body `"type":"cache-not-live"`, wrap as `*cacheNotLiveError`;
otherwise classify 503 as a generic transient (still fallbackable —
5xx without a known code means "supervisor broken, fall back").

**Handler-side change** (scope of enabler bead):

```go
// internal/api/handler_sessions.go (and peer handlers)
func (h *sessionsHandler) List(ctx context.Context, input *ListInput) (*ListOutput, error) {
    if !h.cache.IsLive() {
        return nil, huma.Error503ServiceUnavailable(
            "cache not yet live",
            huma.ErrorDetail{Location: "cache", Value: "not-live"},
        )
    }
    // existing logic
}
```

Huma supports typed error bodies; the `"type": "cache-not-live"`
field is set via the error-detail mechanism the project already
uses. Builder verifies the exact Huma API.

**Why 503, not 425 / 409 / 202:** 503 is the standard "temporarily
unavailable, retry later" code. Reverse proxies and load balancers
already understand it. 425 (Too Early) would be semantically cleaner
but is rarely handled by clients. 409 would mislead — there's no
conflict. 202 (Accepted) is for async operations.

**Interaction with `_cache_age_s` (D5):** the age field is not
present on 503 responses (no data returned). That's correct — the
staleness signal is only meaningful when data flowed.

---

## Sequence diagram — per-command routed read (annotated with decisions)

This diagram elaborates AF-1 from the architecture doc by marking
exactly where each design decision (D1-D8) applies.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant U as User
    participant CLI as cmdSessionList
    participant AR as apiClient (D4 gate)
    participant W as api.Client.ListSessions (D1)
    participant G as genclient
    participant H as sessionsHandler (D8 gate)
    participant C as CachingStore

    U->>CLI: gc session list --json
    CLI->>AR: apiClient(cityPath)
    Note over AR: D4 env-gate: GC_NO_API?
    Note over AR: controllerAlive? bind? allow_mutations?
    AR-->>CLI: *api.Client  (or nil → D3 logRoute "fallback controller-down")
    CLI->>W: ListSessions(ctx, state, template)
    W->>G: GetV0CityByCityNameSessionsWithResponse(...)
    G->>H: GET /v0/city/{city}/sessions
    Note over H: D8 IsLive() gate
    alt cache.IsLive() == true
        H->>C: List(sessionLabel)
        C-->>H: []Bead  + reconcileAgeSeconds
        H-->>G: 200 JSON + _cache_age_s (D5)
    else cache.IsLive() == false
        H-->>G: 503 {"type": "cache-not-live"}
        G-->>W: *cacheNotLiveError
        W-->>CLI: err; ShouldFallback → true
        CLI->>CLI: logRoute(fallback, cache-not-live) (D3)
        CLI->>CLI: openCityStore(...) raw bd
    end
    G-->>W: *http.Response
    W->>W: decodeSessions (D2 translator)
    W-->>CLI: []SessionSummary, nil
    CLI->>CLI: logRoute(api, cache_age_s=3.2) (D3)
    CLI-->>U: JSON with _cache_age_s (D5)
```

---

## Open items for user / pm confirmation

1. **D5 schema stability:** confirm whether any existing `gc …
   --json` consumer enforces a closed JSON schema. If so, the
   `_cache_age_s` envelope addition is breaking and needs a
   deprecation/toggle path.
2. **D8 Huma typed-body API:** builder verifies that Huma v2 in the
   vendored version supports the `"type": "cache-not-live"` body
   shape as drafted. If not, builder proposes equivalent.
3. **D6 cold-path independence:** confirm ga-h6w (this bead) and
   ga-d5y (maintenance) can proceed in parallel — they share no
   files and no runtime state. This design assumes they can.

## Out of scope (deliberately)

- Any new read-endpoint Huma handlers beyond the existing 16
  genclient methods. All targets already have generated clients; no
  new handler work is needed for the basic coverage.
- A `--cache-age-max` CLI flag. Called out in ADR as future work;
  not in this migration.
- Streaming/SSE for long-running read patterns. All 16 are
  point-in-time lists or lookups.

## Handoff notes for pm

- Bead 1 (enabler) **must** land first — it defines contracts the
  subsequent beads consume.
- Each per-file bead (2-9) is independent after 1. Suggest pm
  creates them with explicit `depends_on: <enabler-bead-id>` so the
  dispatcher respects the ordering.
- Every per-file bead description should reference this design doc
  + the architecture doc + the rule, and embed the mandatory test
  row list from D7.
- The enabler bead touches: `internal/api/client.go`,
  `internal/api/types_read.go` (new), `internal/api/decode_*.go`
  (new scaffolding), `internal/api/handler_*.go` (IsLive gate),
  `cmd/gc/apiroute.go` (GC_NO_API + logRoute), one new lint script
  under `scripts/`. Estimated ~400 lines diff, ~300 lines tests.
## PM decomposition summary (closing)

10 child beads created, routed to gascity/builder:
- Enabler: ga-71l (contracts — must land first)
- Per-file: ga-idc (session), ga-6s5 (mail), ga-2o9 (rig),
  ga-06g (convoy), ga-2fr (wait), ga-69s (citystatus+status),
  ga-6q1 (order), ga-gti (beads)

Plan doc: /home/jaword/projects/gascity/docs/plans/ga-h6w-read-path-api-routing.md
Enabler slung + builder mailed (gm-wisp-2xa).
User-confirmation items tracked via mail gm-wisp-y5i to mayor.
Per-file beads unblock automatically once ga-71l closes.
close_reason: Decomposition complete — 10 children routed to gascity/builder; enabler ga-71l slung.

title: Fix: align BEADS_ACTOR with GC_AGENT in named-session env overrides
description:
## Root cause

`BEADS_ACTOR` is set exactly once in the agent-env map, at
`cmd/gc/template_resolve.go:223` as `sessName` (the tmux-safe session
name). For pool/ephemeral sessions this yields `<template>-<beadID>`
(e.g. `designer-lgf-b807`); for named singletons it yields the
sanitized identity (e.g. `gascity--investigator`). Meanwhile the
named-session override paths (`cmd/gc/build_desired_state.go:386-389`
and `cmd/gc/session_reconciler.go:1095-1098`) rewrite `GC_AGENT`,
`GC_ALIAS`, and `GC_TEMPLATE` to the raw identity (e.g.
`gascity/investigator`) but leave `BEADS_ACTOR` pointing at the
still-sanitized `sessName`. Result: in a running named session,
`BEADS_ACTOR != GC_AGENT != GC_TEMPLATE`, and
`bd update <id> --claim` (without `--assignee`) stores the cryptic
sanitized session name as the assignee, breaking tier-1 recovery
(`bd list --assignee="$GC_TEMPLATE"`) and making audit trails
unreadable.

Verified empirically in the investigator session itself:

    BEADS_ACTOR=gascity--investigator        # sanitized sessName
    GC_SESSION_NAME=gascity--investigator    # same (sessName)
    GC_AGENT=gascity/investigator            # raw identity
    GC_ALIAS=gascity/investigator            # raw identity
    GC_TEMPLATE=gascity/investigator         # raw identity
    GC_SESSION_ORIGIN=named

Pool/ephemeral path (`template_resolve.go:216-223`) sets BEADS_ACTOR
and GC_AGENT together to sessName, so they agree there. Manual path
(`internal/session/manager.go:336,385`) uses
`RuntimeEnvWithSessionContext`, which doesn't touch BEADS_ACTOR at
all — BEADS_ACTOR survives only from whatever was in `cfg.Env`
upstream, so manual sessions fall back to `sessName = s-<beadID>`
via the ephemeral template-resolve path when they go through it.

Empirical confirmation of `--claim --assignee` behavior: running
`bd update ga-dre --claim --assignee="gascity/investigator"` from
this session stored `assignee=gascity/investigator` (the
`--assignee` value), NOT `BEADS_ACTOR=gascity--investigator`. So the
pack-prompt fix that spells out `--assignee="$GC_TEMPLATE"` works,
and is the correct interim mitigation. This bug is about making the
default (no explicit `--assignee`) do the right thing, and about
not leaving a cryptic BEADS_ACTOR in audit trails.

## Reproducing test

No committed reproducer yet — the evidence is the live env dump
above plus the two code-path reads. Builder should add an assertion
to an existing phase0 spec test (see Fix spec step 3).

## Fix spec

Step 1 — `cmd/gc/build_desired_state.go`. After the four existing
env overrides in the named-session loop (currently lines 386-389),
add the BEADS_ACTOR override so the invariant `BEADS_ACTOR ==
GC_AGENT` holds for named sessions. Imperative diff:

    tp.Env["GC_TEMPLATE"] = namedSessionBackingTemplate(spec)
    tp.Env["GC_ALIAS"] = identity
    tp.Env["GC_AGENT"] = identity
    tp.Env["GC_SESSION_ORIGIN"] = "named"
+   // Keep BEADS_ACTOR aligned with GC_AGENT for named sessions so
+   // bd claims default to the stable identity, not the sanitized
+   // session_name. See ga-dre.
+   tp.Env["BEADS_ACTOR"] = identity

Step 2 — `cmd/gc/session_reconciler.go`. The live-named-session
reconciler has the same four-line block at lines 1095-1098; add
the identical BEADS_ACTOR line directly below line 1098:

    tp.Env["GC_TEMPLATE"] = namedSessionBackingTemplate(spec)
    tp.Env["GC_ALIAS"] = identity
    tp.Env["GC_AGENT"] = identity
    tp.Env["GC_SESSION_ORIGIN"] = "named"
+   tp.Env["BEADS_ACTOR"] = identity

Step 3 — tests. Extend
`cmd/gc/session_model_phase0_runtime_env_spec_test.go` (or add a
new `_test.go` beside it if that file is spec-locked) with one
table-driven test that builds desired state for a configured named
session and asserts:

    tp.Env["BEADS_ACTOR"] == identity
    tp.Env["BEADS_ACTOR"] == tp.Env["GC_AGENT"]
    tp.Env["BEADS_ACTOR"] == tp.Env["GC_TEMPLATE"]   // for singleton named

And one test for a pool/ephemeral agent that asserts:

    tp.Env["BEADS_ACTOR"] == tp.Env["GC_SESSION_NAME"]
    tp.Env["BEADS_ACTOR"] == tp.Env["GC_AGENT"]

The existing test helpers in that file already build `tp` via
`resolveTemplatePrepared` / `buildDesiredState`; follow the same
shape.

Step 4 — docs. Add a short subsection to
`docs/reference/cli.md` under the session-environment / agent-env
documentation (or create one if it doesn't exist yet) titled
"BEADS_ACTOR convention." Content:

  - `BEADS_ACTOR` is the default actor for `bd` mutations, including
    `bd update <id> --claim` when `--assignee` is omitted.
  - For **named singleton** sessions, `BEADS_ACTOR == GC_AGENT ==
    GC_TEMPLATE == <rig>/<template>` (e.g. `gascity/investigator`).
  - For **pool/ephemeral** sessions, `BEADS_ACTOR == GC_SESSION_NAME
    == <template-basename>-<beadID>` (e.g. `designer-lgf-b807`).
  - For **manual** (`gc session new`) sessions without `--alias`,
    `BEADS_ACTOR == GC_SESSION_NAME == s-<beadID>` (e.g.
    `s-lgf-jtvr`).
  - `bd update --claim --assignee=<X>` uses `<X>` literally; the
    `--assignee` flag overrides `BEADS_ACTOR` for that call. Pack
    prompts pin `--assignee="$GC_TEMPLATE"` for stable routing
    regardless of spawn path.

## Out of scope

- Do **not** change the BEADS_ACTOR format for pool or manual
  sessions. The acceptance criteria only pin the named-singleton
  case to `GC_TEMPLATE`; changing the others is a larger refactor.
- Do **not** refactor `RuntimeEnvWithSessionContext` to also set
  BEADS_ACTOR. Today BEADS_ACTOR is set in one place
  (`template_resolve.go`) and the named overrides run after that,
  so adding the line in the named paths is sufficient. Centralizing
  via a helper is a nice cleanup but belongs in a follow-up.
- Do **not** investigate the "sling to `l1-gc-factory/claude-multiplex/builder`
  didn't auto-spawn" note at the bottom of the bead description —
  the bug author explicitly said to record but not investigate now.
- Do **not** touch `template_resolve.go:223` (the ephemeral path
  already sets BEADS_ACTOR correctly for its case).

## Done-when

- [ ] `cmd/gc/build_desired_state.go` named-session loop sets
      `tp.Env["BEADS_ACTOR"] = identity`.
- [ ] `cmd/gc/session_reconciler.go` `resolveTemplateForLiveNamedSession`
      sets `tp.Env["BEADS_ACTOR"] = identity`.
- [ ] New phase0 runtime-env test asserts `BEADS_ACTOR == GC_AGENT ==
      GC_TEMPLATE` for a named singleton `TemplateParams`.
- [ ] New phase0 test asserts `BEADS_ACTOR == GC_SESSION_NAME ==
      GC_AGENT` for an ephemeral-pool `TemplateParams`.
- [ ] `go test ./cmd/gc/... ./internal/session/...` is green.
- [ ] `docs/reference/cli.md` gains a "BEADS_ACTOR convention"
      subsection per Step 4.
- [ ] Manual smoke: after rebuilding `gc` and restarting a named
      session, `cat /proc/$(pgrep -f '\[... \] <rig>/<template>')/environ
      | tr '\0' '\n' | grep -E '^(BEADS_ACTOR|GC_AGENT|GC_TEMPLATE)='`
      shows the same value for all three.
close_reason: Code change landed as 668eeaa1. Named-singleton BEADS_ACTOR now aligns with GC_AGENT/GC_TEMPLATE. Pool/ephemeral and manual paths unchanged (already correct). Phase0 tests green; full go test ./cmd/gc/... has 5 pre-existing failures unrelated to this change (verified against baseline).

title: Fix: align BEADS_ACTOR with GC_AGENT in named-session env overrides
description:
## Root cause

`BEADS_ACTOR` is set exactly once in the agent-env map, at
`cmd/gc/template_resolve.go:223` as `sessName` (the tmux-safe session
name). For pool/ephemeral sessions this yields `<template>-<beadID>`
(e.g. `designer-lgf-b807`); for named singletons it yields the
sanitized identity (e.g. `gascity--investigator`). Meanwhile the
named-session override paths (`cmd/gc/build_desired_state.go:386-389`
and `cmd/gc/session_reconciler.go:1095-1098`) rewrite `GC_AGENT`,
`GC_ALIAS`, and `GC_TEMPLATE` to the raw identity (e.g.
`gascity/investigator`) but leave `BEADS_ACTOR` pointing at the
still-sanitized `sessName`. Result: in a running named session,
`BEADS_ACTOR != GC_AGENT != GC_TEMPLATE`, and
`bd update <id> --claim` (without `--assignee`) stores the cryptic
sanitized session name as the assignee, breaking tier-1 recovery
(`bd list --assignee="$GC_TEMPLATE"`) and making audit trails
unreadable.

Verified empirically in the investigator session itself:

    BEADS_ACTOR=gascity--investigator        # sanitized sessName
    GC_SESSION_NAME=gascity--investigator    # same (sessName)
    GC_AGENT=gascity/investigator            # raw identity
    GC_ALIAS=gascity/investigator            # raw identity
    GC_TEMPLATE=gascity/investigator         # raw identity
    GC_SESSION_ORIGIN=named

Pool/ephemeral path (`template_resolve.go:216-223`) sets BEADS_ACTOR
and GC_AGENT together to sessName, so they agree there. Manual path
(`internal/session/manager.go:336,385`) uses
`RuntimeEnvWithSessionContext`, which doesn't touch BEADS_ACTOR at
all — BEADS_ACTOR survives only from whatever was in `cfg.Env`
upstream, so manual sessions fall back to `sessName = s-<beadID>`
via the ephemeral template-resolve path when they go through it.

Empirical confirmation of `--claim --assignee` behavior: running
`bd update ga-dre --claim --assignee="gascity/investigator"` from
this session stored `assignee=gascity/investigator` (the
`--assignee` value), NOT `BEADS_ACTOR=gascity--investigator`. So the
pack-prompt fix that spells out `--assignee="$GC_TEMPLATE"` works,
and is the correct interim mitigation. This bug is about making the
default (no explicit `--assignee`) do the right thing, and about
not leaving a cryptic BEADS_ACTOR in audit trails.

## Reproducing test

No committed reproducer yet — the evidence is the live env dump
above plus the two code-path reads. Builder should add an assertion
to an existing phase0 spec test (see Fix spec step 3).

## Fix spec

Step 1 — `cmd/gc/build_desired_state.go`. After the four existing
env overrides in the named-session loop (currently lines 386-389),
add the BEADS_ACTOR override so the invariant `BEADS_ACTOR ==
GC_AGENT` holds for named sessions. Imperative diff:

    tp.Env["GC_TEMPLATE"] = namedSessionBackingTemplate(spec)
    tp.Env["GC_ALIAS"] = identity
    tp.Env["GC_AGENT"] = identity
    tp.Env["GC_SESSION_ORIGIN"] = "named"
+   // Keep BEADS_ACTOR aligned with GC_AGENT for named sessions so
+   // bd claims default to the stable identity, not the sanitized
+   // session_name. See ga-dre.
+   tp.Env["BEADS_ACTOR"] = identity

Step 2 — `cmd/gc/session_reconciler.go`. The live-named-session
reconciler has the same four-line block at lines 1095-1098; add
the identical BEADS_ACTOR line directly below line 1098:

    tp.Env["GC_TEMPLATE"] = namedSessionBackingTemplate(spec)
    tp.Env["GC_ALIAS"] = identity
    tp.Env["GC_AGENT"] = identity
    tp.Env["GC_SESSION_ORIGIN"] = "named"
+   tp.Env["BEADS_ACTOR"] = identity

Step 3 — tests. Extend
`cmd/gc/session_model_phase0_runtime_env_spec_test.go` (or add a
new `_test.go` beside it if that file is spec-locked) with one
table-driven test that builds desired state for a configured named
session and asserts:

    tp.Env["BEADS_ACTOR"] == identity
    tp.Env["BEADS_ACTOR"] == tp.Env["GC_AGENT"]
    tp.Env["BEADS_ACTOR"] == tp.Env["GC_TEMPLATE"]   // for singleton named

And one test for a pool/ephemeral agent that asserts:

    tp.Env["BEADS_ACTOR"] == tp.Env["GC_SESSION_NAME"]
    tp.Env["BEADS_ACTOR"] == tp.Env["GC_AGENT"]

The existing test helpers in that file already build `tp` via
`resolveTemplatePrepared` / `buildDesiredState`; follow the same
shape.

Step 4 — docs. Add a short subsection to
`docs/reference/cli.md` under the session-environment / agent-env
documentation (or create one if it doesn't exist yet) titled
"BEADS_ACTOR convention." Content:

  - `BEADS_ACTOR` is the default actor for `bd` mutations, including
    `bd update <id> --claim` when `--assignee` is omitted.
  - For **named singleton** sessions, `BEADS_ACTOR == GC_AGENT ==
    GC_TEMPLATE == <rig>/<template>` (e.g. `gascity/investigator`).
  - For **pool/ephemeral** sessions, `BEADS_ACTOR == GC_SESSION_NAME
    == <template-basename>-<beadID>` (e.g. `designer-lgf-b807`).
  - For **manual** (`gc session new`) sessions without `--alias`,
    `BEADS_ACTOR == GC_SESSION_NAME == s-<beadID>` (e.g.
    `s-lgf-jtvr`).
  - `bd update --claim --assignee=<X>` uses `<X>` literally; the
    `--assignee` flag overrides `BEADS_ACTOR` for that call. Pack
    prompts pin `--assignee="$GC_TEMPLATE"` for stable routing
    regardless of spawn path.

## Out of scope

- Do **not** change the BEADS_ACTOR format for pool or manual
  sessions. The acceptance criteria only pin the named-singleton
  case to `GC_TEMPLATE`; changing the others is a larger refactor.
- Do **not** refactor `RuntimeEnvWithSessionContext` to also set
  BEADS_ACTOR. Today BEADS_ACTOR is set in one place
  (`template_resolve.go`) and the named overrides run after that,
  so adding the line in the named paths is sufficient. Centralizing
  via a helper is a nice cleanup but belongs in a follow-up.
- Do **not** investigate the "sling to `l1-gc-factory/claude-multiplex/builder`
  didn't auto-spawn" note at the bottom of the bead description —
  the bug author explicitly said to record but not investigate now.
- Do **not** touch `template_resolve.go:223` (the ephemeral path
  already sets BEADS_ACTOR correctly for its case).

## Done-when

- [ ] `cmd/gc/build_desired_state.go` named-session loop sets
      `tp.Env["BEADS_ACTOR"] = identity`.
- [ ] `cmd/gc/session_reconciler.go` `resolveTemplateForLiveNamedSession`
      sets `tp.Env["BEADS_ACTOR"] = identity`.
- [ ] New phase0 runtime-env test asserts `BEADS_ACTOR == GC_AGENT ==
      GC_TEMPLATE` for a named singleton `TemplateParams`.
- [ ] New phase0 test asserts `BEADS_ACTOR == GC_SESSION_NAME ==
      GC_AGENT` for an ephemeral-pool `TemplateParams`.
- [ ] `go test ./cmd/gc/... ./internal/session/...` is green.
- [ ] `docs/reference/cli.md` gains a "BEADS_ACTOR convention"
      subsection per Step 4.
- [ ] Manual smoke: after rebuilding `gc` and restarting a named
      session, `cat /proc/$(pgrep -f '\[... \] <rig>/<template>')/environ
      | tr '\0' '\n' | grep -E '^(BEADS_ACTOR|GC_AGENT|GC_TEMPLATE)='`
      shows the same value for all three.
notes: Duplicate of ga-6fo (same investigator dump, created 4 seconds earlier). Closing to avoid double-work.

title: Fix: compactor order — convert from formula+pool to exec, wire up the actual flatten
description:
## Root cause

`mol-dog-compactor` order is declared as `formula = ... pool = "dog"` but the
formula body carries a **"ZFC Exemption"** section asserting agents can't
execute it — a compactor *daemon* is supposed to, and no such daemon exists
anywhere in `gascity` (grepped `mol-dog-compactor`, `CompactDog`, `PourMolecule`,
etc. under `cmd/`, `internal/`, `examples/` — zero hits in Go code outside tests).

Effect on user's city (`gc-management`):

- Order fired 4 times on 2026-04-13/14/15/16. Last firing's cycle bead
  `gm-z4m9crf` ("mol-dog-compactor") is **still OPEN** since 2026-04-16
  (6 days ago as of 2026-04-22), with 5 open child task beads.
- Because the cycle bead never closed, the order is blocked from firing
  again.
- Meanwhile, `mol-dog-reaper` (which was already converted formula+pool →
  exec — see its own `# Converted from formula+pool to exec` header
  comment) has fired 166× over the same window and completes on schedule.
- With no compactor running, `gm` DB ballooned to 9.9 GB (~47k issues).
  User worked around it manually via dump → DROP DATABASE → re-import
  (reclaimed 94% / 9.3 GB), but that procedure is fragile and undocumented.

## Files involved

**Source of the broken order — this is what you change:**

- `gascity/examples/dolt/orders/mol-dog-compactor.toml:1-6`
  (currently: `formula = "mol-dog-compactor"` / `pool = "dog"` /
  `trigger = "cooldown"` / `interval = "24h"`)
- `gascity/examples/dolt/formulas/mol-dog-compactor.toml` (wrapper)
- `gascity/examples/dolt/formulas/mol-dog-compactor.formula.toml`
  (the formula body, with the "ZFC Exemption" section)

**Working model to copy — exec-based order:**

- `gascity/examples/gastown/packs/maintenance/orders/mol-dog-reaper.toml`
  (projected copy lives in the user's city at
  `gc-management/.gc/system/packs/maintenance/orders/mol-dog-reaper.toml`
  — shape is `exec = "$PACK_DIR/assets/scripts/reaper.sh"` + cooldown)
- `gascity/examples/gastown/packs/maintenance/assets/scripts/reaper.sh`
  — pattern for enumerating user DBs and iterating. Also the inline SQL
  pattern using `dolt_sql` and table filtering conventions.

**Primitives that already exist and do the work:**

- `bd flatten` (beads rig) — implements the Tim Sehn
  soft-reset/commit/swap recipe via `CALL DOLT_*` stored procs. Driver
  at `beads/internal/storage/versioncontrolops/flatten.go:22-70`.
  Landed 2026-03-25 (beads `5e0e83ab`), server-mode-safe since
  `c4fbe1ea`.
- `bd compact --dolt` (beads rig) — selective squash preserving recent
  commits. Not needed for this fix but present for reference.

**Additional dependency:**

- Compactor script needs `dolt-target.sh` (port/host discovery). Currently
  lives in the maintenance pack at
  `examples/gastown/packs/maintenance/assets/scripts/dolt-target.sh`.
  The `dolt` pack does not currently import `maintenance`. Pick whichever
  matches existing pack-dependency conventions:
  (a) copy the small script into `dolt/assets/scripts/`,
  (b) add an import dependency on `maintenance`, or
  (c) inline the few lines of port discovery.
  The reaper uses `. "$SCRIPT_DIR/dolt-target.sh"` — keep the same API.

## Reproducing test

Functional repro against the user's city (read-only observation):

```
grep '"type":"order.fired".*mol-dog-compactor' \
  /home/jaword/projects/gc-management/.gc/events.jsonl | wc -l
  # → 4 (last on 2026-04-16)

bd --rig gm list --label 'order-run:mol-dog-compactor' --status=open
  # → gm-z4m9crf (5 open children), stuck for 6 days

grep -c '"type":"order.fired".*mol-dog-reaper' \
  /home/jaword/projects/gc-management/.gc/events.jsonl
  # → 166 — reaper fires on schedule because it's exec-based
```

Acceptance test to add (integration/acceptance suite):

1. Stand up a sandbox city with the dolt pack.
2. Seed a sandbox DB with `> $threshold` commits (default 500).
3. Trigger the order manually (`gc order run mol-dog-compactor` or wait
   for cooldown).
4. Assert within N seconds:
   - The order's exec exit code is 0.
   - `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM dolt_log` on the seeded DB drops to 1.
   - Row counts on every user table are unchanged (integrity check —
     this is the safety guarantee the original formula described).
   - Next firing cycle is not blocked.

## Fix spec

### 1. Convert the order (small, mechanical)

Rewrite `gascity/examples/dolt/orders/mol-dog-compactor.toml` to the
exec shape, mirroring the reaper header comment for provenance:

```toml
# Converted from formula+pool to exec. All compactor operations are
# deterministic: SQL resets, commits, and CALL dolt_gc. No LLM judgment
# needed — runs inline in the controller.
[order]
description = "Flatten Dolt commit history to reduce storage"
exec = "$PACK_DIR/assets/scripts/compactor.sh"
trigger = "cooldown"
interval = "24h"
```

Delete (or tombstone per project norm):

- `examples/dolt/formulas/mol-dog-compactor.toml`
- `examples/dolt/formulas/mol-dog-compactor.formula.toml`

If the files are embedded via `examples/dolt/embed.go`, make sure the
embed directive still compiles after removal (no dangling entries).

### 2. Add `examples/dolt/assets/scripts/compactor.sh`

Model after `reaper.sh`. Rough structure:

```sh
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# compactor — flatten Dolt history on each user DB to reclaim storage.
#
# Replaces mol-dog-compactor formula. Uses the Tim Sehn flatten recipe
# (soft-reset to root, commit, swap main) via CALL DOLT_* stored procs.
# The DOLT_CHECKOUT/RESET/COMMIT calls are session-stateful — run them
# as one multi-statement query so they share a session.
set -euo pipefail

# Resolve dolt_sql helper (port/host discovery). Either source from
# maintenance pack or duplicate its handful of lines.
. "$(dirname "$0")/dolt-target.sh"

THRESHOLD="${GC_COMPACTOR_THRESHOLD:-500}"
DRY_RUN="${GC_COMPACTOR_DRY_RUN:-}"

DATABASES=$(dolt_sql -r csv -q "SHOW DATABASES" 2>/dev/null \
    | tail -n +2 \
    | grep -vi '^information_schema$\|^mysql$\|^dolt_cluster$\|^__gc_probe$\|^dolt$' \
    || true)
[ -z "$DATABASES" ] && exit 0

for DB in $DATABASES; do
    COMMITS=$(dolt_sql -r csv -q "USE \`$DB\`; SELECT COUNT(*) FROM dolt_log" \
        2>/dev/null | tail -1 || echo 0)
    [ "$COMMITS" -le "$THRESHOLD" ] && continue

    # Pre-flight row counts for integrity check (excluding dolt_* system tables).
    # Capture into a per-DB temp file to compare post-flatten.

    [ -n "$DRY_RUN" ] && { echo "would flatten $DB ($COMMITS commits)"; continue; }

    # Flatten recipe in ONE dolt_sql invocation (single session required).
    dolt_sql <<SQL
USE \`$DB\`;
SET @initial := (SELECT commit_hash FROM dolt_log ORDER BY date ASC LIMIT 1);
CALL DOLT_BRANCH('flatten-tmp');
CALL DOLT_CHECKOUT('flatten-tmp');
CALL DOLT_RESET('--soft', @initial);
CALL DOLT_COMMIT('-Am', 'compaction: flatten history');
CALL DOLT_CHECKOUT('main');
CALL DOLT_RESET('--hard', 'flatten-tmp');
CALL DOLT_BRANCH('-D', 'flatten-tmp');
CALL DOLT_GC();
SQL

    # Post-flight integrity check: row counts unchanged, commit count == 1.
    # On mismatch → escalate: gc mail send mayor/ -s "ESCALATION: ... [CRITICAL]"; exit 1.
done
```

Notes for the builder:

- Keep the script idempotent: same-cycle re-run should be a no-op
  (commits below threshold → skip).
- Flatten is **safe with concurrent writes** per the original formula's
  own claim ("merge base shifts but diff is just the txn"). Do NOT add
  `gc suspend`/`gc resume` in this patch — if live-write safety turns
  out to be wrong, a cfg toggle can follow.
- The DOLT_CHECKOUT mutation only affects the compactor's own session.
  Other concurrent sessions keep their current branch.
- Match the reaper's defensive style (`|| true`, `2>/dev/null`) so a
  single-DB failure doesn't abort the whole cycle.
- `DOLT_GC()` cannot run inside an explicit transaction (matches
  `beads/internal/storage/versioncontrolops/gc.go:8-16`). Keep it as
  the last statement in the same script but outside any BEGIN.
- Empty or already-flat DBs: when `COUNT(*) FROM dolt_log == 1`, skip.

### 3. No beads-rig change needed

`bd flatten` already does this at the per-DB level. We intentionally
**do not** shell out to `bd flatten` from the script because:

- `bd flatten` picks DB from the caller's `.beads/config.yaml` (cwd),
  which forces `cd` into each rig's beads dir.
- The dolt server in a typical gastown city hosts several DBs (the
  user's city has `gm`, `be`, `gascity`, `mc`, `sfi`,
  `projectwrenunity`) — iterating via `dolt sql` against each DB on the
  server is a closer match to how `reaper.sh` already works.
- Keeps the order self-contained (no `bd` binary dependency on the
  controller host).

If the beads team wants a `bd squash` alias for `bd flatten`, that's a
separate trivial PR against `beads/cmd/bd/flatten.go`. Not required
here.

### 4. No dolt-rig change needed

All primitives used (`DOLT_BRANCH`, `DOLT_CHECKOUT`, `DOLT_RESET --soft`,
`DOLT_COMMIT`, `DOLT_GC`) are existing stored procedures in
`dolt/go/libraries/doltcore/sqle/dprocedures/init.go`. The user's prior
attempt at `CALL dolt_checkout('--orphan')` is unsupported — confirmed
via `dolt/go/cmd/dolt/cli/arg_parser_helpers.go:184-193`, which
registers only `-b`, `-B`, `-f`, `-t`, and `--overwrite-ignore`. The
soft-reset path we use does not need orphan support.

## Out of scope

- `bd squash` alias in beads rig (see §3 above).
- `gc suspend`/`gc resume` integration around the flatten (see §2 note).
- Surgical mode (preserve recent N commits via interactive rebase). The
  original formula defined a surgical mode too — carry it over only if
  trivial; flatten alone resolves the immediate bloat. A separate bead
  can add surgical.
- Telemetry / structured report output beyond what reaper currently
  emits.

## Done-when

- [ ] `examples/dolt/assets/scripts/compactor.sh` added, executable,
      passes `shellcheck` and the gascity lint rules that apply to
      `reaper.sh`.
- [ ] `examples/dolt/orders/mol-dog-compactor.toml` rewritten in the
      exec shape above.
- [ ] Formula files removed (or tombstoned per project norm).
- [ ] `examples/dolt/embed.go` still compiles after file changes.
- [ ] Acceptance test (per §Reproducing test) added and green.
- [ ] `go test ./...` green under packages touched.
- [ ] PR opened and merged in `gastownhall/gascity`.
- [ ] **Operator note in PR description** — after merge, the user must
      close the stuck cycle bead in their live city to unblock
      scheduling. Do NOT automate from CI; it's a one-time recovery:

      ```
      cd /home/jaword/projects/gc-management
      for id in gm-z4m9crf gm-z4m9crf.1 gm-z4m9crf.2 \
                gm-z4m9crf.3 gm-z4m9crf.4 gm-z4m9crf.5; do
        bd close "$id" --reason "superseded: compactor order converted to exec"
      done
      ```

## Routing notes for the builder

- Rig: `gascity` (the order and script files are source in
  `gascity/examples/dolt/`, `//go:embed`-ed into the `gc` binary via
  `examples/dolt/embed.go`).
- No beads PR, no dolt PR. Single-rig change.
- Full verification requires the user to rebuild and run `gc` in their
  city — include the operator recovery command in the PR description.
notes:
Implementation complete:
- examples/dolt/assets/scripts/compactor.sh: new exec script with flatten recipe, dry-run, integrity check, escalation to mayor on row-count divergence
- examples/dolt/orders/mol-dog-compactor.toml: converted to exec order
- examples/dolt/formulas/mol-dog-compactor.toml: deleted
- examples/dolt/compactor_test.go: 6 unit tests (projected target, managed port fallback, below-threshold skip, flatten above threshold, dry-run, order shape)
- examples/dolt/assets/scripts/runtime.sh: guard GC_DOLT_PORT under set -u

Test results: all 6 compactor tests green; examples/dolt (11s) and examples/gastown (13s) green; TestBuiltinPackIncludes* green.

Unrelated pre-existing cmd/gc failures (TestCmdOrderHistory/Run*ProviderAware) confirmed on baseline via git stash.

title: Architecture: bd/dolt query latency (~5s) gates all gc reads
description:
Follow-up from ga-amh (gc session list slowness).

## Finding

Every `bd` subprocess call against the city's dolt store takes 4-7 seconds on the current gc-management instance. This is independent of which query shape is asked:

- `bd list --json --label=gc:session` (8 beads returned): 4.75s
- `bd list --json` (221 beads returned): 7.01s
- `bd show --json <id>` (1 bead returned): 5.51s
- `bd list --json --limit 5`: 4.86s

The dolt data directory at `/home/jaword/projects/gc-management/.beads/dolt` is **11 GB** despite storing only 221 issues. This is likely several orders of magnitude of accumulated history relative to the live dataset.

Because bd invocations cost this much, gc session list --json hits a hard ~5s floor no matter how the CLI is rewritten — every status-like command is bounded by a single bd list query.

## Decision needed

The ga-amh acceptance criteria asked for <500ms on gc session list --json. The PR I just opened (#1128) fixes the amplifier (redundant bd/tmux calls per session, 192s → 5s for non-JSON), but it cannot cross the ~5s floor without one of:

1. Store maintenance: dolt gc / archive old history on the managed dolt server. Operational, not code. Would need to be a rigged-in periodic task or manual procedure with a runbook.
2. Supervisor-side status cache: a long-lived process maintains a read model of session state on disk / memory and serves CLI reads from it (e.g. a --cached flag, or the CLI reads the cache when fresh and falls back to bd when stale). New abstraction with its own invalidation rules and failure modes — architect call.
3. Different schema for status: move status-frequently-read fields out of Dolt into a lighter read path (flatfile snapshot, SQLite, etc.). Also an architect call.

All three are outside an investigator's authority — they require a call on what the system should look like, not just a fix to what it is.

## Evidence

- PR #1128 for the amplifier fix: https://github.com/gastownhall/gascity/pull/1128
- Dolt directory size: du -sh /home/jaword/projects/gc-management/.beads/dolt → 11G
- Live issue count: bd list --json --limit 0 | jq length → 221

## Out of scope here

- The per-session redundancy in cmd_session.go / session_reconcile.go is already in #1128; not this bead's concern.
notes:
architect-1 investigation 2026-04-22:

Confirmed and extended the investigator's findings:

1. **Physical store**: .beads/dolt/gm/.dolt/noms/oldgen = 9.8 GB of noms chunks for ~64 issues. Dolt history dominates the store.
2. **Reproduced latency**: `bd list --json --label=gc:session --include-infra --include-gates --limit 0` against the managed gm Dolt server: 5.4-6.0s wall time, consistent. Directly matches gc session list --json floor.
3. **Existing infrastructure worth leveraging**:
   - internal/beads/caching_store.go already wraps BdStore with an in-memory cache + event-driven invalidation (PrimeActive, Prime, StartReconciler). Used by the controller via cmd/gc/api_state.go:wrapWithCachingStore.
   - internal/api (Huma) already exposes /v0/sessions backed by that CachingStore (humaHandleSessionList). Reads come from in-memory state when cache is live.
   - cmd/gc/apiroute.go:apiClient(cityPath) already routes CLI commands to the supervisor's localhost API when the controller is alive, with ShouldFallback-based graceful fallback to raw bd.
   - The apiClient+fallback pattern is already used by: cmd_session submit, cmd_suspend, cmd_agent (pool set/get), cmd_rig (add/update). Mutations.
4. **The gap**: cmdSessionList at cmd/gc/cmd_session.go:515 opens the raw BdStore directly. It does NOT try apiClient first. Same likely holds for other read-path siblings (peek/wake/get, status, convoy/mail reads) — none were changed by PR #1128.

Architecture conclusion: the three options in the bead collapse to TWO parallel decisions. No new abstraction required.

- ADR: read-path CLI commands route through supervisor API (reuses existing CachingStore; bounds gc session list --json well under 500ms when supervisor is up; gracefully falls back to raw bd when down). This is "option 2" but as *wiring*, not a new cache.
- ADR: dolt store maintenance runbook (periodic dolt gc on the managed server + guardrail against orphan probe dbs). This is "option 1"; bounds the cold-path floor (supervisor startup, hooks, operator bd invocations, test fixtures).
- Option 3 (separate schema) NOT pursued — not needed if 1+2 are in place.
architect-1 outputs 2026-04-22:

Architecture doc:
- docs/architecture/gc-read-path.md (FR/NFR, data model erDiagram, 3 use-case flowcharts, 2 sequenceDiagrams with prose, security/integrations/risks tables, alternatives)

ADRs:
- docs/adr/0001-gc-read-path-routes-through-supervisor.md
- docs/adr/0002-dolt-store-maintenance-runbook.md

Guardrail rules:
- docs/rules/gc-read-path-routes-through-api.md
- docs/rules/dolt-store-maintenance.md

adr_sync (actual adr-bot --no-tailor):
- ran successfully against /home/jaword/projects/gascity
- server reports 'No ADRs available for this stack yet' for go/cobra
- result: no managed CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md emitted; locally-authored ADRs in docs/adr/ are the source of truth.

git commit: skipped. Working tree is on user feature branch fix/799-control-dispatcher-pool-resume-flags and no user.name/user.email configured locally. Files left in working tree (untracked) for downstream agents and the user to commit on a clean branch when appropriate.

Children ga-h6w (ADR 0001 read-path routing) and ga-d5y (ADR 0002 dolt maintenance) created with needs-design label and slung to gascity/designer. See ga-1f9 notes for output paths and adr-bot sync result.

title: PR 1026: address Julian's review comments
description:
GitHub PR: https://github.com/gastownhall/gascity/pull/1026

Julian requested changes with three points (see the PR comments for full text):

1. **Major**: the new stale-sibling warning leaks into opportunistic resolveCity() fallback paths. Unrelated commands emit the warning when probing outside a city. Fix: suppress the warning when failOnLoadError=false, OR move emission up to callers that explicitly depend on registered-rig resolution.

2. **Minor**: warning reporting lives inside registeredRigBindings via a package-global writer. Bypasses command-owned stderr, can duplicate the same warning in one command, forces tests to override global state. Fix: route diagnostics through caller-owned output or structured results; emit each stale entry at most once per command.

3. **Minor**: the stale-entry tolerance is a Stat-then-load check — TOCTOU race. If sibling city.toml disappears between Stat and loadCityConfigSuppressDeprecatedOrderWarnings, ENOENT still lands in loadErrors and aborts. Fix: handle os.ErrNotExist on the actual config-load path and add regression coverage.

Branch is whichever PR 1026 points at; discover with 'gh pr view 1026 --repo gastownhall/gascity --json headRefName'. Push to same branch. Do NOT merge. Reply on GitHub when done.
acceptance_criteria: 1) All three review points addressed with code changes + tests in the feature branch. 2) Tests pass locally. 3) Branch pushed to origin. 4) Reply comment posted on PR 1026 enumerating each point and the commit that addressed it. 5) Bead closed with PR URL and commit shas in notes.
notes: PR 1026: addressed all 3 review points in commit 269453d6 (pushed to fork). Replied on GitHub: https://github.com/gastownhall/gascity/pull/1026#issuecomment-4300195278
